additionalTime: 30 minutes to 12 hours marinating plus 20 minutes beef chill time
author: Robert Carr
description: Korean BBQ bulgogi with thin-sliced beef, Asian pear, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, sesame, lightly charred vegetables, and a reserved glaze for finishing in the skillet.
equipment: Large cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or flat-top griddle

groceryIngredients.item: boneless ribeye, top sirloin, or skirt steak
butter lettuce or red leaf lettuce

ingredients: 2 pounds boneless ribeye, top sirloin, or skirt steak, chilled until firm and sliced across the grain 1/8 inch thick, or Korean-market pre-sliced bulgogi beef
1 medium Asian pear or Bosc pear, peeled and cored, using 1/2 cup finely grated pear and juice
1 medium yellow onion, half finely grated for 1/4 cup onion and juice and half thinly sliced for cooking
6 garlic cloves, finely grated or minced
1 tablespoon peeled fresh ginger, finely grated
6 green onions, 4 thinly sliced for the marinade and 2 thinly sliced for garnish
8 ounces fresh shiitake or cremini mushrooms, stems trimmed and caps sliced 1/4 inch thick
1 medium carrot, peeled and cut into thin matchsticks
1/2 cup low-sodium soy sauce
3 tablespoons packed light brown sugar
2 tablespoons honey
2 tablespoons mirin or rice wine
1 tablespoon rice vinegar
2 teaspoons gochujang
1 tablespoon plus 1 teaspoon toasted sesame oil, divided
2 tablespoons toasted sesame seeds, divided
1 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground if possible
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or a small pinch Morton kosher salt, for the vegetables
2 tablespoons neutral high-heat oil, such as avocado, grapeseed, canola, or vegetable oil, divided
1 1/2 cups uncooked short-grain white rice, cooked for serving
1 head butter lettuce or red leaf lettuce, leaves separated, optional for wraps
16 ounces kimchi, optional for serving
instructions: If the 2 pounds beef is not already thin-sliced, place it on a plate or small sheet pan and freeze it for 20 minutes, just until the outside is firm but not frozen solid.

Slice the chilled 2 pounds beef across the grain into pieces about 1/8 inch thick. If any slices are wider than 2 inches, cut them into shorter bite-size strips so they cook quickly and are easy to pick up with rice or lettuce.
Finely grate enough peeled pear to measure 1/2 cup packed pear and juice.
Cut the yellow onion in half, finely grate one half until you have about 1/4 cup onion and juice, and thinly slice the remaining half for cooking.

In a large mixing bowl, whisk together the 1/2 cup grated pear and juice, 1/4 cup grated onion and juice, 1/2 cup low-sodium soy sauce, 3 tablespoons packed light brown sugar, 2 tablespoons honey, 2 tablespoons mirin or rice wine, 1 tablespoon rice vinegar, 2 teaspoons gochujang, 1 tablespoon toasted sesame oil, 6 finely grated garlic cloves, 1 tablespoon finely grated ginger, 1 teaspoon black pepper, 4 thinly sliced green onions, and 1 tablespoon toasted sesame seeds.

Before the raw beef goes into the bowl, spoon 1/4 cup of the clean marinade into a small bowl and stir in the remaining 1 teaspoon toasted sesame oil. Set this reserved glaze aside for finishing the cooked bulgogi.
Add the sliced beef to the large bowl with the remaining marinade.
Use clean hands or tongs to separate the beef slices and massage the marinade through the meat for 1 to 2 minutes so every slice is coated.
Cover the bowl and refrigerate the beef for at least 30 minutes and up to 12 hours. For more seasoning without the pear making the meat too soft, aim for 2 to 6 hours.

While the beef marinates, trim and slice the 8 ounces mushrooms, cut the 1 medium carrot into thin matchsticks, thinly slice the remaining half onion, thinly slice the remaining 2 green onions for garnish, and cook the 1 1/2 cups short-grain white rice if serving with rice.
Take the marinated beef out of the refrigerator 15 minutes before cooking so it loses its chill but stays cool.
Set a large plate or sheet pan near the stove for cooked batches.
Heat a large cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, or flat-top griddle over medium-high to high heat for 4 to 5 minutes, until a drop of water sizzles hard and evaporates quickly.
Add 1 tablespoon neutral high-heat oil to the hot pan.
Add the sliced half onion, 8 ounces sliced mushrooms, 1 carrot cut into matchsticks, and 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt.
Cook the vegetables for 3 to 4 minutes, stirring every 30 to 45 seconds, until the onion softens, the mushrooms brown at the edges, and the carrot is crisp-tender.
Transfer the vegetables to the large plate or sheet pan.
Add 1 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil to the pan and let it shimmer for 15 to 20 seconds.
Lift about one-third of the beef from the marinade with tongs, let the excess marinade drip back into the bowl for a few seconds, and spread the beef into a single layer in the hot pan.
Cook the first batch for 60 to 90 seconds without stirring, until the underside is browned in spots.
Flip and stir the beef for 60 to 90 seconds more, until there are no raw red spots and the edges are caramelized.
Drizzle 1 tablespoon of the reserved clean glaze over the cooked batch, toss for 10 to 15 seconds to coat, then transfer the beef to the plate with the vegetables.
Repeat with the second and third batches of beef, adding 1 teaspoon neutral high-heat oil before each batch and using 1 tablespoon reserved clean glaze for each batch.
Discard the raw marinade left in the large bowl after the beef is removed.
Return all cooked beef and vegetables to the hot pan.
Add any remaining reserved clean glaze and toss everything together for 30 to 45 seconds, just until the bulgogi is hot and lightly sticky.
Turn off the heat and sprinkle the bulgogi with the remaining 1 tablespoon toasted sesame seeds and the remaining 2 thinly sliced green onions.
Serve immediately over the cooked short-grain rice, with separated lettuce leaves and kimchi on the side if using.
notes: For easier prep, buy Korean-market pre-sliced bulgogi beef or ask the butcher to slice ribeye, top sirloin, or skirt steak across the grain about 1/8 inch thick.
Ribeye gives the richest, most tender result. Top sirloin is leaner and still works well if sliced thin. Skirt steak has a stronger beef flavor but must be sliced across the grain.
Asian pear adds sweetness and helps tenderize the beef. Bosc pear works as a substitute. Avoid using a large amount of kiwi because it can make thin beef mushy.
Use low-sodium soy sauce. If using regular soy sauce, reduce the soy sauce to 1/3 cup and add 2 tablespoons water to keep the bulgogi from turning too salty.
The reserved glaze must be spooned out before raw beef touches the marinade. Do not use raw leftover marinade as a finishing sauce.
Crowding is the main reason bulgogi turns watery. Cook in 3 batches so the beef browns and caramelizes instead of steaming.
A very hot cast-iron skillet, carbon-steel skillet, grill pan, or flat-top griddle helps the beef brown indoors.
The vegetables are cooked first so they brown instead of steaming under wet beef. Keep them crisp-tender because they will warm again at the end.
Serve with short-grain rice for a bowl, or spoon the bulgogi into lettuce leaves with rice and kimchi for wraps.
Leftovers keep for 3 days in the refrigerator. Reheat quickly in a hot skillet with a splash of water, just until warmed through.
title: Korean BBQ Bulgogi
totalTime: 50 minutes plus 30 minutes to 12 hours marinating
